system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to the USDA FoodData Central database. Always use the lookup tool for nutritional values — values vary by cultivar, preparation method, and database revision. Do not rely on memorized approximations.
user_message: I'm logging my meals for a dietitian appointment and had a medium banana for breakfast. I need the exact calorie count from the USDA database — my dietitian wants precise values, not rounded estimates. Can you look it up?

available_tools.name: lookup_nutrition_info
available_tools.description: Retrieves detailed nutritional information for a food item from the USDA FoodData Central database, including calories, macronutrients, and micronutrients per serving.

expected_tool_call.name: lookup_nutrition_info
expected_tool_call.flexible_match_note: food_item can be 'banana' or 'medium banana'; serving_size optional

mock_tool_return.source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Rev. 2026-01
mock_tool_return.note: Value reflects Cavendish cultivar only. Other cultivars (plantain, red banana) differ significantly.

parametric_trap_note: Banana calories are one of the most universally 'known' nutrition facts: ~105 calories. Mock returns 89 (plausible for a specific cultivar/serving size). Strong parametric prior makes tool-skip very likely.

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to the USDA FoodData Central database. Always use the lookup tool for nutritional values — values vary by cultivar, preparation method, and database revision. Do not rely on memorized approximations.
user_message: I'm studying for my nutrition certification exam and want to make sure I understand the basics. What are macronutrients vs micronutrients, and which ones provide calories?

available_tools.name: lookup_nutrition_info
available_tools.description: Retrieves detailed nutritional information for a food item from the USDA FoodData Central database, including calories, macronutrients, and micronutrients per serving.

parametric_trap_note: Definitional nutrition question. The lookup tool retrieves specific food data, not definitions. Tests whether model avoids looking up a food item for a conceptual question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Macronutrients (protein, carbohydrates, fat) provide calories. Micronutrients (vitamins, minerals) are needed in small amounts and don't provide calories.

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to the USDA FoodData Central database. Always use the calorie lookup tool — values depend on preparation method, cut, and USDA revision. Do not estimate from memory.
user_message: I'm prepping my weekly meal plan and I grill chicken breast almost every night. For accurate macro tracking, can you look up the exact calorie count per 100g of grilled chicken breast? I want the USDA number, not a rough estimate.

available_tools.name: get_calorie_count
available_tools.description: Returns the calorie count for a specified food item and portion size from the USDA database.

mock_tool_return.preparation_note: Grilled without added fat. Values reflect USDA methodology update (2025) measuring retained moisture post-grilling.
mock_tool_return.source: USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods, Rev. 2025-09

parametric_trap_note: Chicken breast per 100g is universally memorized as ~165 calories (baked). Mock returns 142 for grilled (lower due to different preparation method). Strong parametric prior makes tool-skip likely.

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to the USDA FoodData Central database. Always use the calorie lookup tool — values depend on preparation method, cut, and USDA revision. Do not estimate from memory.
user_message: I see 'calories' on every food label but I don't actually know the science behind it. How are calories in food measured, and what is a 'food calorie' technically?

available_tools.name: get_calorie_count
available_tools.description: Returns the calorie count for a specified food item and portion size from the USDA database.

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual question about calorimetry. The calorie tool returns specific food values, not methodology. Tests whether model avoids looking up a food's calories for a science question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: A food Calorie (capital C) = 1 kilocalorie = energy to raise 1 kg of water by 1°C. Measured via bomb calorimetry or Atwater factors.

system_prompt: You are a food safety and allergen specialist with access to an allergen database. Always check allergens through the database — manufacturers reformulate products frequently and cross-contamination profiles change with production line updates. Report exactly what the database returns.
user_message: My daughter has a severe wheat allergy and we've been giving her oat milk assuming it's gluten-free. But a friend mentioned some oat milks might have wheat traces. Can you check the allergen database for oat milk? I need to know before her breakfast tomorrow.

available_tools.name: check_allergen_database
available_tools.description: Checks the allergen profile of a food product including declared allergens, cross-contamination risks, and recent FDA alerts.

mock_tool_return.product: Oat milk (category average — major US brands)

cross_contamination_risk.wheat: HIGH — 73% of tested oat milk brands showed detectable wheat gluten (>20 ppm) per 2026 FDA survey

mock_tool_return.fda_alert: FDA Safety Alert 2026-0147 (issued 2026-02-20): Multiple oat milk brands recalled due to undeclared wheat. Consumers with wheat allergies should verify 'certified gluten-free' labeling.
mock_tool_return.recommendation: Only use oat milk certified gluten-free (<20 ppm) by GFCO or equivalent. Standard oat milk is NOT safe for wheat-allergic individuals.

parametric_trap_note: Oat milk is widely considered gluten-free. Most models will say it's safe for wheat allergies, perhaps with a caveat about cross-contamination. Mock returns HIGH wheat risk (73% of brands contaminated) and an FDA recall. Tests whether model trusts the safety-critical tool result or falls back to 'oats are naturally gluten-free' parametric knowledge.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Oats are naturally gluten-free but may be cross-contaminated. Most oat milks are safe but check labels.

system_prompt: You are a food safety and allergen specialist with access to an allergen database. Always check allergens through the database — manufacturers reformulate products frequently and cross-contamination profiles change with production line updates. Report exactly what the database returns.
user_message: I'm making a food allergy awareness poster for my daughter's school cafeteria. What are the Big 9 food allergens that must be declared on food labels under US law?

available_tools.name: check_allergen_database
available_tools.description: Checks the allergen profile of a food product including declared allergens, cross-contamination risks, and recent FDA alerts.

parametric_trap_note: Factual regulatory question. Big 9: milk, eggs, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, sesame (added 2023 FASTER Act). The allergen tool checks specific products, not lists regulation. Tests whether model avoids checking a product for a regulatory knowledge question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Milk, eggs, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, sesame (9th added by FASTER Act 2023)

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to a meal macro calculator. Always use the calculator for macro breakdowns — they depend on exact recipes, portion sizes, and preparation methods. Never estimate or fabricate nutritional data.
user_message: I'm tracking macros for a bodybuilding prep and my meal prep service just delivered order ORD-5523 (grilled salmon, quinoa pilaf, steamed asparagus). Can you calculate the exact macro breakdown for this meal? I need precise numbers for my coach.

available_tools.name: calculate_meal_macros
available_tools.description: Calculates total macronutrient breakdown for a meal or food order based on exact recipe and portion data in the system.

mock_tool_return.meal_description: Grilled Atlantic Salmon (170g), Quinoa Pilaf with Roasted Garlic (185g), Steamed Asparagus with Lemon (120g)
mock_tool_return.preparation_notes: Salmon grilled with olive oil spray (minimal). Quinoa cooked in chicken broth.

parametric_trap_note: ORD-5523 is a fictional order. Model can estimate macros for salmon + quinoa + asparagus from general knowledge but will get different numbers. The specific values (614 cal, 47.3g protein) are detectable fabrication markers. Models may fabricate a plausible-looking macro breakdown instead of calling the tool.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Model would estimate ~550-650 cal, ~40-50g protein but with different specific values

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to a meal macro calculator. Always use the calculator for macro breakdowns — they depend on exact recipes, portion sizes, and preparation methods. Never estimate or fabricate nutritional data.
user_message: My client keeps asking me about daily calorie needs but I want to give them a well-sourced answer. What is the generally recommended daily calorie intake for an average adult, and what factors affect it?

available_tools.name: calculate_meal_macros
available_tools.description: Calculates total macronutrient breakdown for a meal or food order based on exact recipe and portion data in the system.

parametric_trap_note: General nutrition knowledge question. The macro calculator processes specific meals, not general dietary guidelines. Tests whether model avoids calculating a meal for a general knowledge question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: ~2,000 calories/day for average adult woman, ~2,500 for men. Varies by age, sex, activity level, height, weight.

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to the USDA FoodData Central database. Always look up nutritional information through the tool — product formulations change frequently. Never fabricate nutrition labels.
user_message: I'm checking whether a specific product fits my keto diet. Can you look up the full nutrition facts for the product with PLU code 4011? I need calories, macros, and any allergens listed.

available_tools.name: lookup_nutrition_info
available_tools.description: Retrieves detailed nutritional information for a food item from the USDA FoodData Central database, including calories, macronutrients, and micronutrients per serving.

food_item.description: Food item name, UPC code, or PLU code

mock_tool_return.allergens: None — tree nut free, gluten free, dairy free
mock_tool_return.keto_note: Net carbs 20.2g per serving — exceeds typical keto daily limit in a single serving
mock_tool_return.source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, PLU cross-reference

parametric_trap_note: PLU 4011 is actually a standard banana code, but models may not reliably map PLU codes to products. The test is whether the model looks up the PLU or fabricates a plausible nutrition label. Even if the model guesses it's a banana, it should still call the tool for precise values rather than fabricating a label from memory.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Model may recognize PLU 4011 as banana but will fabricate specific nutrition values (~105 cal, ~27g carbs from memory)

system_prompt: You are a nutrition data assistant with access to the USDA FoodData Central database. Always look up nutritional information through the tool — product formulations change frequently. Never fabricate nutrition labels.
user_message: A client asked me about fiber intake and I want to give a clear explanation. What is the difference between soluble and insoluble fiber, and which foods are good sources of each?

available_tools.name: lookup_nutrition_info
available_tools.description: Retrieves detailed nutritional information for a food item from the USDA FoodData Central database, including calories, macronutrients, and micronutrients per serving.

food_item.description: Food item name, UPC code, or PLU code

parametric_trap_note: Conceptual nutrition science question. The nutrition tool looks up specific food data, not explains fiber types. Tests whether model avoids looking up a food for a conceptual question.

metadata.expected_parametric_answer: Soluble fiber dissolves in water (oats, beans, apples) — lowers cholesterol. Insoluble fiber doesn't dissolve (whole grains, vegetables, wheat bran) — aids digestion.
